1: Praise awaits you in silence, O God, in Zion — this is a harvest thanksgiving psalm associated with Zion, possibly connected to one of the three annual pilgrimage festivals (Passover, Weeks/Shavuot, or Tabernacles/Sukkot — Deuteronomy 16:16). The agricultural thanksgiving of vv 9-13 best fits Shavuot (wheat harvest) or Tabernacles (the ingathering of all crops). The psalm bears both "Psalm" and "Song" designations. "Vow shall be fulfilled" (yeshullam neder) — the vow made in distress is publicly paid at the temple upon the prayer's answer.
2: To you all flesh shall come — the universality of prayer in Zion theology. While the Torah's cultic system specified who could enter which courts of the sanctuary, the prophets envisioned Zion as the prayer-destination of all peoples (Isaiah 56:7: "my house shall be called a house of prayer for all peoples"; Isaiah 2:2-4: "all nations shall stream to it"). "All flesh" (kol-basar) is the most comprehensive possible term for humanity, used in Gen 6-9 for all living creatures. YHWH who hears prayer is accessible to all humanity, not only to Israel's covenant members.
3: When our sins overwhelm us, you atone for our transgressions — the prerequisite for temple access is divine atonement for sin. The worshiper who comes to Zion carrying accumulated transgressions cannot simply enter the divine presence; atonement must precede access. This is the function of the sacrificial system and the Day of Atonement (Lev 16) in the priestly theology: YHWH provides the mechanism of atonement that makes his own presence accessible. The note here is entirely divine initiative — "you atone" (tekhapper), not the worshiper's self-purification.
4: Blessed is the one you choose and bring near, to dwell in your courts — the beatitude form used for divine election to temple access. In the priestly system, only Levites could serve in the sanctuary; the high priest alone could enter the Holy of Holies once per year. But the prophets expanded this theology: Isaiah 56:3-7 explicitly extends temple-access to foreigners and eunuchs who might have been excluded under some readings of the Torah. This verse's "chosen and brought near" anticipates that broader vision — the one YHWH selects for nearness is blessed regardless of prior status.
5: You are the hope of all the ends of the earth and of the most distant seas — the cosmic scope of YHWH's saving reach. "The ends of the earth" (qetze ha-aretz) and "the most distant seas" (yam rehokkim) are standard formulas for the geographic extremes of the known world. In the Second Temple period, Jewish diaspora communities stretched from Egypt to Babylon to Persia, and the question of YHWH's accessibility to dispersed Israel far from Zion was a live theological concern (Solomon's prayer in 1 Kings 8:41-43 explicitly addresses foreigners and those who pray toward the temple from far away).
6: The one who by his strength sets the mountains in place, who is clothed with power — the mountains as evidence of divine power over creation. In ANE cosmology, mountains were understood as the pillars supporting the vault of the sky (Job 26:11; Psalms 75:3) and as the dwelling places of the gods (Baal's Mount Zaphon; YHWH's Mount Sinai and Zion). The God who "sets" the mountains in place — who established them at creation — is the same God who controls the agricultural cycle of vv 9-13. The psalm moves from cosmic creative power to the intimate detail of watering furrows.
7: You still the roaring of the seas, the roaring of their waves, and the uproar of the nations — the parallelism of the seas' roaring and the nations' tumult is not merely poetic. In ANE cosmology (and in Psalms 2:1; Psalms 46:3; Psalms 93:3-4; Isaiah 17:12-13; Revelation 17:15), the chaos-waters and the tumultuous nations are related symbols: both represent powers of disorder that oppose divine order. YHWH who controls the physical chaos of the storm-sea also governs the historical chaos of rebellious nations. Creation-theology and history-theology are one unified framework in the Psalter.
8: Those who live at earth's farthest bounds stand in awe of your signs — the cosmic portents (otot, signs) that YHWH performs are visible to those at the geographic extremes. The concept of signs that produce yirah (fear/awe) at a distance is rooted in the Exodus tradition (the plagues as signs — Exodus 10:1-2; the Rahab narrative — Joshua 2:9-10, where those at a distance heard and were terrified). The phrase "you make the sunrise and sunset ring with joy" personifies the dawn and dusk as the boundaries of each day's divine proclamation.
9: You visit the earth and water it abundantly; you enrich it with the river of God — the rain cycle as covenant blessing and implicit Baal polemic. Baal was the Canaanite storm-and-rain deity; the entire Baal cult cycle was predicated on the claim that Baal's annual death and resurrection controlled the agricultural calendar. Ps 65's harvest thanksgiving attributes every element of the rain cycle to YHWH directly: he visits the earth, waters it, fills the channels, provides the grain. The "river of God" (nahar elohim) is the heavenly reservoir of water (Genesis 1:6-8; Psalms 104:13) from which YHWH draws to send rain. YHWH does what Baal's worshipers claimed Baal did.
10: You soak its furrows abundantly, you level its ridges, you soften it with showers — the agricultural detail is remarkable for its intimacy and specificity: furrows plowed for planting, ridges between rows, the softening rain that prepares the seedbed. The God who set the mountains in place (v6) also tends the individual furrow. This theological move — from cosmic creation-power to agricultural intimacy — is characteristic of the Psalter's creation theology (compare Ps 104, which moves from light and stars to the feeding of young ravens). YHWH is not an absentee cosmic deity but the intimate sustainer of the agricultural cycle.
11: You crown the year with your bounty; your wagon tracks overflow with abundance — the harvest is personified as YHWH's processional wagon, overflowing with grain as it passes. The image of YHWH's wagon-tracks dripping with fat abundance portrays the divine journey through the landscape as leaving behind a trail of fertility. This processional imagery connects to the Feast of Tabernacles and to the ark processions (Psalms 68:7-8, 17-18), where YHWH's movement through the land brings blessing. The harvest is not an impersonal agricultural outcome but the aftermath of divine presence passing through.
12: The pastures of the wilderness overflow; the hills are wrapped in rejoicing — even the uninhabited wilderness areas (midbar) receive the divine bounty. The "pastures of the wilderness" are the steppe areas used for grazing flocks but not for agriculture; YHWH's blessing reaches beyond the cultivated farmland to the pastoral economy as well. "The hills are wrapped in rejoicing" (gevot tachgornah, the hills gird themselves with joy) — the imagery of hills putting on joy as a garment, personification that prepares for the full personification of the landscape in v13.
13: The meadows are dressed with flocks; the valleys are blanketed with grain — they shout for joy, they break into song — the entire landscape is personified as the worshipping community. The flocks clothing the meadows and grain blanketing the valleys are images of abundance so complete that the landscape itself joins the human festival. This personification of creation as a worshipping assembly connects to Psalms 96:11-12 ("let the heavens be glad, let the earth rejoice") and to the eschatological vision of Isaiah 55:12 where "the mountains and hills will burst into song before you and all the trees of the field will clap their hands."
